era_context: Late 14th-century Europe during the Hundred Years' War; the Ottoman Empire expanding in the Balkans; Ming Dynasty consolidating control under the Hongwu Emperor; the Papacy returning to Rome from Avignon after nearly 70 years in France. This is a period of military conflict, political centralization, and religious transition.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (minimal contemporary records)

events.title: Return of the Papacy to Rome; Pope Gregory XI dies
events.description: Pope Gregory XI, who had returned the papal court to Rome in 1377 after nearly 70 years of papal residence in Avignon, died on March 27, 1377. Gregory XI's return to Rome was a major event, ending the so-called Avignon Papacy and reestablishing papal authority in Rome and the Papal States. However, Gregory XI's death immediately created a succession crisis. The College of Cardinals, under pressure from the Roman mob and the Roman nobility, elected Urban VI (Bartolomeo Prignano) as the new pope in April 1378. Gregory XI's death and the subsequent election of Urban VI set the stage for the Western Schism, which would erupt later in 1378.
key_figures: Pope Gregory XI (Pierre Roger de Beaufort, died March 27, 1377)

sources.name: Dietrich of Nieheim and other contemporary chroniclers

events.certainty_note: Gregory XI's death and the return of the papacy to Rome are well-documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles. The date of his death (March 27, 1377) is confirmed.
cross_references: Leads to 1378-001 (Urban VI's election and Western Schism)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor consolidates Ming control; administrative reforms
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued his consolidation of Ming power through administrative reforms and the elimination of potential rivals. In 1377, he implemented reforms that strengthened central control over the provinces and reduced the power of regional military commanders. These reforms were part of his broader effort to centralize authority and prevent the emergence of regional warlords. The administrative structure being established in 1377 would form the basis of Ming governance for centuries.
events.certainty_note: Ming court records document administrative reforms in 1377. The date and general nature of the reforms are well-established.

events.title: Ottoman forces expand in the Balkans
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I continued their expansion in the Balkans, consolidating control over territories in what is now Bulgaria, Greece, and Albania. In 1377, Ottoman forces were engaged in multiple campaigns against Serbian, Bulgarian, and Venetian forces. The exact details of specific battles and sieges in 1377 are often unclear, as Ottoman sources are primarily later compilations. However, by 1377, the Ottoman Empire had clearly established itself as the dominant military power in the Balkans and was continuing its systematic conquest of the region.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans is well-documented in broad outline, but specific events in 1377 are less clearly dated. Ottoman sources are primarily later compilations.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English military campaigns and diplomacy
events.description: English forces, under the command of various nobles, continued military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations in France as part of the Hundred Years' War. In 1377, English forces were engaged in raids and sieges in Aquitaine and other regions of southwestern France. The campaigns of 1377 were part of the broader pattern of English military activity during this period, though the war had entered a phase of relative stalemate compared to the major victories of the 1350s–1360s. The death of King Edward III in 1377 marked a transition in English leadership, with his young grandson Richard II becoming king.
key_figures: King Richard II (England, age 10, became king in 1377)
events.certainty_note: The death of Edward III on June 21, 1377, is well-documented. The Hundred Years' War is well-documented, though specific campaigns in 1377 are less clearly detailed.
cross_references: Leads to 1380-001 (poll tax crisis under Richard II)

events.title: Timur consolidates power in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Turco-Mongol conqueror, was consolidating his power in Central Asia in 1377. By this year, Timur had established himself as a major power in the region and was preparing for his major campaigns of conquest that would begin in the 1380s. The exact chronology of Timur's early rise is uncertain, as sources are primarily later compilations, but by 1377 he was clearly emerging as a significant power in Central Asia.

sources.name: Persian and Central Asian chronicles (Rashid al-Din, others)

events.certainty_note: Timur's early rise to power is documented in later Persian and Central Asian sources. The exact chronology is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The return of the papacy to Rome is sometimes presented as a triumphant restoration of papal authority, but modern historians emphasize that Gregory XI's return was politically motivated and that the papacy's position in Rome remained precarious. The Western Schism is sometimes attributed to Urban VI's personality alone, but modern scholarship emphasizes structural and political factors. The death of Edward III is sometimes presented as marking a clear transition in English history, but modern historians note the continuity of English policies and the gradual nature of change. Ottoman expansion is sometimes presented as inevitable, but recent scholarship notes the resistance and agency of Balkan states.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1377 is rich for Western Europe (England, France, Papal States) and moderately good for the Ottoman Empire and Ming China. However, Ottoman and Central Asian sources are primarily later compilations, introducing dating uncertainty. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Americas are essentially absent from contemporary written records. The return of the papacy to Rome and Gregory XI's death are extensively documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles. The death of Edward III is well-documented in English and French chronicles. The historical record for 1377 is shaped by the survival of written records in literate, centralized states; oral traditions and non-literate societies are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Gregory XI's death led to Urban VI's election and the Western Schism
Return to Rome ended the Avignon Papacy (1309–1377)
Part of Ottoman expansion in Balkans leading to Battle of Kosovo
Death of Edward III and accession of Richard II led to poll tax crisis in 1380
